front.title: API-Key Session Credentials for the Agent Enrollment Protocol

abstract.t: This document defines an API-key session-credential extension for the Agent Enrollment Protocol (AEP). The extension lets an AEP Service issue an opaque API key through the AEP Grant command for deployments that already operate header-based API-key authentication.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: AEP session credentials allow a Service to issue a stateful credential after an Agent authenticates with a baseline AEP client assertion . This document defines the api-key grant type for Services that want to reuse existing API-key middleware while preserving AEP key possession as the issuance root. Extension request and response bodies are JSON objects carried over HTTP semantics as defined by AEP.
This extension does not replace baseline AEP authentication. Services that implement this extension MUST continue to accept baseline AEP authentication on authenticated AEP commands.

section.name: Requirements Language
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Grant Type
section.t: The grant type identifier is:
A Service that supports this extension lists api-key in commands.grant_types and lists grant and revoke in commands.supported in its AEP Inspect document.

section.name: Inspect Configuration
section.t: A Service MAY publish configuration under commands.grant_types_config.api-key:
default_lifetime_seconds is an AEP-owned numeric value and is therefore represented as a JSON string.

header_names, when present, lists HTTP header names the Service can accept for API-key presentation. Header names are case-insensitive on the wire, but Services SHOULD publish lowercase names. If absent, the default header name is x-api-key.
scopes_supported, when present, lists Service-defined scope strings an Agent can request.
supports_per_credential_revoke is a string boolean. If absent, the default is "false".

section.name: Grant Request
section.t: The Agent invokes AEP Grant using baseline Authorization: AEP <jwt> authentication with op equal to grant.
grant_type MUST be api-key.
label is OPTIONAL and is an Agent-provided display label. Services MAY ignore it.
requested_scopes is OPTIONAL. A Service MAY grant fewer scopes than requested.

section.name: Grant Response
section.t: A successful Grant response is a JSON object:
api_key is REQUIRED and contains the opaque API key value.
header is REQUIRED and identifies the HTTP header used for presentation. When header_names is advertised, the value MUST be one of the advertised names.
expires_at is REQUIRED and is an RFC 3339 timestamp for credential expiry.
scopes is REQUIRED and contains the granted scope strings. The Service MAY return an empty array when the API key has no scope-limited authorization.
credential_id, when present, is a stable identifier for per-key Revoke.
Services MUST issue expiring API keys.

section.name: Credential Presentation
section.t: On later HTTP requests, the Agent presents the API key in the response-selected header:
The same endpoint MUST continue to accept baseline AEP authentication.

section.name: Revoke
section.t: The Agent invokes AEP Revoke using baseline Authorization: AEP <jwt> authentication with op equal to revoke.
To revoke all API keys of this type for the authenticated Agent:
To revoke one API key when the Service returned credential_id:
Revoke returns an empty JSON object on success. The Service MUST return success regardless of whether a matching key existed.
To revoke all session credentials of every grant type, Agents use the core all_grant_types Revoke request.

section.name: Error Handling
section.t: This extension uses the AEP error vocabulary defined by the core protocol. An API key that is expired, malformed, revoked, unknown, or bound to a different Agent fails as not_recognized.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document requests registration of api-key in the AEP Grant Types registry.

texttable.c: Opaque API key issued through AEP Grant

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: API keys are bearer secrets. Services MUST store only salted hashes or equivalent one-way verifiers. Services MUST NOT log raw API-key values, and Services MUST support AEP Revoke for every advertised grant type. Agents that suspect key disclosure SHOULD call AEP Revoke using baseline AEP authentication and then fall back to per-request signed client assertions until a new key is issued.

Services MUST validate only the configured API-key header for this extension. Services SHOULD reject ambiguous requests that present multiple API-key headers or multiple non-baseline bearer credentials when that ambiguity would affect authorization semantics.

section.name: Privacy Considerations
section.t: API keys can become correlation handles if reused outside the issuing Service. Agents MUST NOT present AEP-issued API keys to other Services. Services MUST NOT log raw API-key values in ordinary logs or telemetry.

front.title: Date and Time on the Internet: Timestamps

abstract.t: This document defines a date and time format for use in Internet protocols that is a profile of the ISO 8601 standard for representation of dates and times using the Gregorian calendar.

front.title: The JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) Data Interchange Format

abstract.t: JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) is a lightweight, text-based, language-independent data interchange format. It was derived from the ECMAScript Programming Language Standard. JSON defines a small set of formatting rules for the portable representation of structured data.
This document removes inconsistencies with other specifications of JSON, repairs specification errors, and offers experience-based interoperability guidance.

The Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) is a stateless application-level protocol for distributed, collaborative, hypertext information systems. This document describes the overall architecture of HTTP, establishes common terminology, and defines aspects of the protocol that are shared by all versions. In this definition are core protocol elements, extensibility mechanisms, and the "http" and "https" Uniform Resource Identifier (URI) schemes.
This document updates RFC 3864 and obsoletes RFCs 2818, 7231, 7232, 7233, 7235, 7538, 7615, 7694, and portions of 7230.

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels

abstract.t: In many standards track documents several words are used to signify the requirements in the specification. These words are often capitalized. This document defines these words as they should be interpreted in IETF documents. This document specifies an Internet Best Current Practices for the Internet Community, and requests discussion and suggestions for improvements.

front.title: Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words

abstract.t: RFC 2119 specifies common key words that may be used in protocol specifications. This document aims to reduce the ambiguity by clarifying that only UPPERCASE usage of the key words have the defined special meanings.
